commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content:
Unless you be circumcised. Many who had been converted from Judaism, held that none, not even converted from paganism, could be saved, unless they were circumcised, and observed the other ceremonies of the law of Moses. (Witham)
See Galatians v. 2.
commentaries.content_en:
Unless you be circumcised. Many who had been converted from Judaism, held that none, not even converted from paganism, could be saved, unless they were circumcised, and observed the other ceremonies of the law of Moses. (Witham)
See Galatians v. 2.
commentaries.content_pt:
A menos que sejais circuncidados. Muitos que haviam sido convertidos do judaísmo sustentavam que nenhum, nem mesmo os convertidos do paganismo, poderia ser salvo, a menos que fossem circuncidados e observassem os outros rituais da lei de Moisés. (Witham)
Veja Gálatas v. 2.

ai_summary.one_sentence: Haydock discute a necessidade da circuncisão para a salvação segundo a Lei de Moisés.
ai_summary.abstract: George Leo Haydock, em seu comentário de 1849, aborda a controvérsia sobre a circuncisão e sua relação com a salvação, especialmente entre os convertidos do judaísmo. Ele menciona que muitos acreditavam que a salvação era impossível sem a observância da Lei de Moisés, incluindo a circuncisão. Este debate é central em Gálatas 5:2, onde Paulo argumenta contra a imposição da Lei sobre os gentios convertidos, enfatizando a liberdade em Cristo. Haydock destaca a importância de entender a relação entre a Lei e a graça, refletindo sobre a transição do judaísmo para o cristianismo.
key_points: A circuncisão era vista como essencial para a salvação por alguns convertidos do judaísmo.
A Lei de Moisés incluía rituais que eram considerados obrigatórios.
Gálatas 5:2 é um versículo chave na discussão sobre a liberdade em Cristo.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A salvação não depende da circuncisão ou da observância da Lei de Moisés.
arguments: A circuncisão foi uma prática do Antigo Testamento, mas não é necessária para a salvação no Novo Testamento.
Paulo argumenta que a fé em Cristo é suficiente para a salvação, independentemente da observância da Lei.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: A imposição da circuncisão contradiz a mensagem da graça e da liberdade em Cristo.

spiritual_insight.theme: A liberdade em Cristo transcende as obrigações da Lei.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: A verdadeira salvação é encontrada na fé em Cristo, não em rituais ou tradições humanas.

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Such were the pains they took in their love of power: and it was not with the knowledge of the Apostles that they Paul and Barnabas were blamed. But still they brought forward none of these charges: but when they have proved the matter, then (the Apostles) write in stronger terms. For gentleness is everywhere a great good: gentleness, I say, not stupid indifference; gentleness, not adulation: for between these there is a vast difference. Nothing ruffled Paul, nothing discomposed Peter. When you have convincing proofs, why lose your temper, to render these of none effect. It is impossible for one who is out of temper ever to persuade. Yesterday also we discoursed about anger; but there is no reason why we should not today also; perchance a second exhortation coming directly after the first will effect somewhat. For indeed a medicine though of virtue to heal a wound, unless it be constantly renewed, mars all. And think not that our continual discoursing about the same things is a condemning of you: for if we condemned you, we should not discourse; but now, hoping that you will gain much, we speak these things. Would indeed that we did speak constantly of the same things: would that there were no other subject of our discourses, than how we might overcome our passions. For is it not contrary to all reason, that while emperors, living in luxury and so great honor, have no subject of discourse either while sitting at table, or at any other time, save only how to overcome their enemies — and therefore it is that they hold their assemblies each day, and appoint generals and soldiers, and demand taxes and tributes; and that of all state affairs, the moving causes are these two, the overcoming of those who make war upon them, and the establishing of their subjects in peace— we have no mind for such themes as this, nor ever even dream of conversing upon them: but how we may buy land, or purchase slaves, and make our property greater, these are subjects we can talk about every day, and never be tired of them: while concerning things in ourselves and really our own, we neither wish to speak ourselves, nor so much as dream of tolerating advice, nor of enduring to hear others speaking about them. But answer me, what do you talk about. About dinner. Why that is a subject for cooks. Nay, that is a theme for hucksters and merchants. Of buildings. That belongs to carpenters and builders. That talk is for husbandmen. But for us, there is no other proper business, save this, how we may make wealth for the soul. Then let not the discourse be wearisome to you. Why is it that none finds fault with the physician for always discoursing of the healing art, nor with people of other crafts for talking about their peculiar arts. If indeed the mastery over our passions were really achieved, so that there were no need of putting us in mind, we might reasonably be taxed with ambition and display: or rather, not then either. For even if it were gained, for all that, there would be need of discoursing, that one might not relapse and remain uncorrected: as in fact physicians discourse not only to the sick, but also to the whole, and they have books on this subject, on the one part how to free from disease, on the other how to preserve health. So that even if we are well, still we must not give over, but must do all in order to the preserving of our health. And when we are sick there is a twofold necessity for advice: first, that we may be freed from the disease; secondly, that having been freed, we may not fall into it again. Well then, we are discoursing now by the method of treating the sick, not by the rules for the treatment of the healthy. How then may one root out this evil passion. How subdue (ὑ ποσκελίσειε) this violent fever. Let us see whence it had its birth, and let us remove the cause. Whence is it wont to arise. From arrogance and much haughtiness. This cause then let us remove, and the disease is removed together with it. But what is arrogance. Whence does it arise. For perhaps we are likely to have to go back to a still higher origin. But whatever course the reason of the thing may point out, that let us take, that we may go to the bottom of the mischief, and pluck it up by the roots. Whence then comes arrogance. From our not looking into our own concerns, but instead of that, busying ourselves about the nature of land, though we are not husbandmen, and the nature of gold, though we are not merchants, and concerning clothing, and everything else: while to ourselves and our own nature we never look at all. And who, you will say, is ignorant of his own nature. Many: perhaps all, save a few: and if you will, I will show the proof of it. For, tell me, what is man. If one were asked, will he be able to answer outright to the questions, In what he differs from the brutes, in what he is akin to the heavenly inhabitants, what can be made of man. For as in the case of any other material, so also in this case: man is the subject-matter, but of this can be made either an angel or a beast. Does not this seem a strange saying. And yet you have often heard it in the Scriptures. For of certain human beings it was said, he is the angel of the Lord Malachi 2:7: and from his lips, says it, they shall seek judgment Malachi 3:1: and again, I send My angel before Your face: but of some, Serpents, generation of vipers. Matthew 12:34 So then, it all depends upon the use. Why do I say, an angel. The man can become God, and a child of God. For we read, I have said, You are gods, and all of you are children of the Most High. Psalm 82:6 And what is greater, the power to become both God and angel and child of God is put into his own hands. Yea, so it is, man can be the maker of an angel. Perchance this saying has startled you. Hear however Christ saying: In the Resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels. Matthew 22:30 And again, He that is able to receive it, let him receive it. Matthew 19:12 In a word, it is virtue which makes angels: but this is in our power: therefore we are able to make angels, though not in nature, certainly in will. For indeed if virtue be absent, it is no advantage to be an angel by nature; and the Devil is a proof of this, who was an angel once: but if virtue be present, it is no loss to be a man by nature; and John is a proof of this, who was a man, and Elias who went up into heaven, and all those who are about to depart there. For these indeed, though with bodies, were not prevented from dwelling in heaven: while those others, though without bodies, could not remain in heaven. Let no one then grieve or be vexed with his nature as if it were a hindrance to him, but with his will. He (the Devil) from being incorporeal became a lion: for lo. It says, Our adversary, as a roaring lion, walks about, seeking whom he may devour 1 Peter 5:8: we from being corporeal, become angels. For just as if a person, having found some precious material, should despise it, as not being an artificer, it will be a great loss to him, whether it be pearls, or a pearl shell, or any other such thing that he has seen; so we likewise, if we are ignorant of our own nature, shall despise it much: but if we know what it is, we shall exhibit much zeal, and reap the greatest profits. For from this nature is wrought a king's robe, from this a king's house, from this nature are fashioned a king's members: all are kingly. Let us not then misuse our own nature to our hurt. He has made us a little lower than the angels, Psalm 8:5, I mean, by reason of death: but even that little we have now recovered. There is nothing therefore to hinder us from becoming near to the angels, if we will. Let us then will it, let us will it, and having exercised ourselves thoroughly, let us return honor to the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit, now and ever, world without end, Amen.

commentaries.content_pt: Tal foram os esforços que fizeram em seu amor pelo poder: e não foi com o conhecimento dos Apóstolos que eles culparam Paulo e Barnabé. Mas ainda assim, não apresentaram nenhuma dessas acusações: mas quando provaram a questão, então (os Apóstolos) escreveram em termos mais fortes. Pois a mansidão é em toda parte um grande bem: mansidão, digo, não indiferença estúpida; mansidão, não adulação: pois entre estas há uma vasta diferença. Nada perturbou Paulo, nada descompôs Pedro. Quando você tem provas convincentes, por que perder a calma, tornando essas provas ineficazes? É impossível para alguém que está fora de si persuadir. Ontem também discutimos sobre a ira; mas não há razão para que não possamos hoje também; talvez uma segunda exortação, vindo diretamente após a primeira, tenha algum efeito. Pois, de fato, um remédio, embora tenha virtude para curar uma ferida, a menos que seja constantemente renovado, prejudica tudo. E não pense que nossa contínua discussão sobre as mesmas coisas é uma condenação de vocês: pois se nós os condenássemos, não discutiríamos; mas agora, esperando que vocês ganhem muito, falamos essas coisas. Queria de fato que falássemos constantemente sobre as mesmas coisas: quisesse que não houvesse outro assunto em nossos discursos, senão como poderíamos superar nossas paixões. Pois não é contrário a toda razão que, enquanto imperadores, vivendo em luxo e tão grande honra, não tenham outro assunto de discurso, seja enquanto estão à mesa, ou em qualquer outro momento, senão como superar seus inimigos — e é por isso que realizam suas assembleias todos os dias, e nomeiam generais e soldados, e exigem impostos e tributos; e que de todos os assuntos de estado, as causas motivadoras são estas duas, a superação daqueles que fazem guerra contra eles, e o estabelecimento de seus súditos em paz — nós não temos interesse em temas como este, nem mesmo sonhamos em conversar sobre eles: mas como podemos comprar terras, ou adquirir escravos, e aumentar nossa propriedade, esses são assuntos sobre os quais podemos falar todos os dias, e nunca nos cansar deles: enquanto em relação às coisas em nós mesmos e realmente nossas, nem desejamos falar, nem sonhamos em tolerar conselhos, nem em suportar ouvir outros falando sobre elas. Mas responda-me, sobre o que você fala. Sobre o jantar. Por que esse é um assunto para cozinheiros. Não, esse é um tema para mercadores e comerciantes. Sobre construções. Isso pertence a carpinteiros e construtores. Essa conversa é para lavradores. Mas para nós, não há outro negócio apropriado, exceto este, como podemos fazer riqueza para a alma. Então não deixe que o discurso seja cansativo para você. Por que é que ninguém critica o médico por sempre discutir sobre a arte da cura, nem as pessoas de outras profissões por falarem sobre suas artes peculiares. Se de fato o domínio sobre nossas paixões fosse realmente alcançado, de modo que não houvesse necessidade de nos lembrar, poderíamos ser razoavelmente acusados de ambição e ostentação: ou melhor, nem mesmo então. Pois mesmo que fosse conquistado, ainda assim, haveria necessidade de discutir, para que não recaíssemos e permanecêssemos sem correção: como de fato os médicos discutem não apenas com os doentes, mas também com os saudáveis, e eles têm livros sobre esse assunto, de um lado como libertar da doença, do outro como preservar a saúde. Assim, mesmo que estejamos bem, ainda não devemos desistir, mas devemos fazer tudo para preservar nossa saúde. E quando estamos doentes, há uma dupla necessidade de conselho: primeiro, que possamos ser libertados da doença; em segundo lugar, que, tendo sido libertados, não caiamos novamente nela. Bem, então, estamos discutindo agora pelo método de tratar os doentes, não pelas regras para o tratamento dos saudáveis. Como então pode alguém erradicar essa paixão maligna? Como submeter (ὑ ποσκελίσειε) essa febre violenta? Vamos ver de onde teve seu nascimento, e vamos remover a causa. De onde costuma surgir? Da arrogância e de muita altivez. Essa causa então vamos remover, e a doença é removida junto com ela. Mas o que é arrogância? De onde ela surge? Pois talvez tenhamos que voltar a uma origem ainda mais elevada. Mas qualquer que seja o curso que a razão da coisa possa indicar, esse devemos tomar, para que possamos chegar ao fundo do mal e arrancá-lo pela raiz. De onde então vem a arrogância? De não olharmos para nossos próprios assuntos, mas em vez disso, ocupando-nos com a natureza da terra, embora não sejamos lavradores, e a natureza do ouro, embora não sejamos comerciantes, e sobre vestuário, e tudo mais: enquanto para nós mesmos e nossa própria natureza nunca olhamos. E quem, você dirá, é ignorante de sua própria natureza? Muitos: talvez todos, exceto alguns poucos: e se você quiser, eu mostrarei a prova disso. Pois, diga-me, o que é o homem? Se alguém fosse perguntado, será que ele seria capaz de responder diretamente às perguntas, em que ele difere dos brutos, em que ele é semelhante aos habitantes celestiais, o que pode ser feito do homem? Pois assim como no caso de qualquer outro material, assim também neste caso: o homem é a matéria-prima, mas disso pode ser feito tanto um anjo quanto uma besta. Isso não parece uma afirmação estranha? E ainda assim você já a ouviu muitas vezes nas Escrituras. Pois de certos seres humanos foi dito, ele é o anjo do Senhor Malaquias 2:7: e de seus lábios, diz, eles buscarão juízo Malaquias 3:1: e novamente, eu envio Meu anjo diante de Seu rosto: mas de alguns, Serpentes, geração de víboras. Mateus 12:34 Então, tudo depende do uso. Por que digo, um anjo? O homem pode se tornar Deus, e um filho de Deus. Pois lemos, eu disse, vocês são deuses, e todos vocês são filhos do Altíssimo. Salmo 82:6 E o que é maior, o poder de se tornar tanto Deus quanto anjo e filho de Deus está em suas próprias mãos. Sim, assim é, o homem pode ser o criador de um anjo. Talvez essa afirmação tenha te surpreendido. Ouça, no entanto, Cristo dizendo: Na Ressurreição, eles nem casam, nem são dados em casamento, mas são como os anjos. Mateus 22:30 E novamente, aquele que é capaz de recebê-lo, que o receba. Mateus 19:12 Em uma palavra, é a virtude que faz anjos: mas isso está em nosso poder: portanto, somos capazes de fazer anjos, embora não na natureza, certamente na vontade. Pois de fato, se a virtude estiver ausente, não é vantagem ser um anjo por natureza; e o Diabo é uma prova disso, que foi um anjo uma vez: mas se a virtude estiver presente, não é uma perda ser um homem por natureza; e João é uma prova disso, que foi um homem, e Elias que subiu ao céu, e todos aqueles que estão prestes a partir para lá. Pois estes, de fato, embora com corpos, não foram impedidos de habitar no céu: enquanto aqueles outros, embora sem corpos, não puderam permanecer no céu. Que ninguém então se entristeça ou se irrite com sua natureza como se fosse um obstáculo para ele, mas com sua vontade. Ele (o Diabo) de incorpóreo tornou-se um leão: pois eis que diz, nosso adversário, como um leão rugindo, anda ao redor, buscando a quem possa devorar 1 Pedro 5:8: nós, de corpóreo, nos tornamos anjos. Pois assim como se uma pessoa, tendo encontrado algum material precioso, o desprezasse, por não ser um artífice, será uma grande perda para ele, seja pérolas, ou uma concha de pérola, ou qualquer outra coisa semelhante que ele tenha visto; assim também nós, se ignorarmos nossa própria natureza, a desprezaremos muito: mas se soubermos o que é, exibiremos muito zelo e colheremos os maiores lucros. Pois dessa natureza é feito um manto real, dessa natureza uma casa real, dessa natureza são formados os membros de um rei: todos são reais. Não devemos então abusar de nossa própria natureza para nosso prejuízo. Ele nos fez um pouco inferiores aos anjos, Salmo 8:5, quero dizer, por causa da morte: mas mesmo esse pouco já recuperamos. Portanto, não há nada que nos impeça de nos tornarmos próximos dos anjos, se quisermos. Então, que queiramos, que queiramos, e, tendo nos exercitado completamente, que possamos devolver honra ao Pai, ao Filho e ao Espírito Santo, agora e sempre, pelos séculos dos séculos, Amém.

ai_summary.one_sentence: João Crisóstomo exorta à reflexão sobre a natureza humana e a superação das paixões.
ai_summary.abstract: Neste comentário, João Crisóstomo enfatiza a importância da mansidão e da reflexão sobre a natureza humana em contraste com a busca por poder e riqueza. Ele argumenta que a verdadeira riqueza reside na virtude e no domínio sobre as paixões, e critica a indiferença em relação ao autoconhecimento. Através de uma analogia com médicos que discutem constantemente sobre a saúde, ele sugere que a luta contra a arrogância e a altivez deve ser um tema central em nossas vidas. A natureza humana é apresentada como uma matéria-prima que pode ser moldada para se tornar tanto angélica quanto bestial, dependendo da virtude que cultivamos.
key_points: A mansidão é um bem essencial e deve ser cultivada.
A reflexão sobre a própria natureza é crucial para o autoconhecimento.
A luta contra as paixões deve ser um tema constante de discussão.
A natureza humana pode ser moldada pela virtude ou pela arrogância.
A verdadeira riqueza é espiritual, não material.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A verdadeira riqueza e dignidade do ser humano estão na virtude e no domínio sobre as paixões.
arguments: A mansidão é superior à indiferença e à adulação.
A reflexão contínua sobre a natureza humana é necessária para evitar a arrogância.
A luta contra as paixões deve ser uma prioridade, assim como a saúde é para os médicos.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: Portanto, devemos nos empenhar em cultivar a virtude e o autoconhecimento para alcançar nosso pleno potencial.

spiritual_insight.theme: A virtude é a verdadeira riqueza da alma.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Devemos dedicar tempo à reflexão sobre nossas paixões e buscar a virtude como um caminho para a verdadeira felicidade.

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Mark how at every step of the right progress in respect of the Gentiles, the beginning is brought in as matter of necessity. Before this (Peter) being found fault with, justified himself, and said all that he said in the tone of apology, which was what made his words acceptable: then, the Jews having turned away, upon this (Paul) came to the Gentiles. Here again, seeing another extravagance coming in, upon this (the apostle) enacts the law. For as it is likely that they, as being taught of God, discoursed to all indifferently, this moved to jealousy them of the Jews (who had believed). And they did not merely speak of circumcision, but they said, You cannot even be saved. Whereas the very opposite to this was the case, that receiving circumcision they could not be saved. Do you mark how closely the trials succeed each other, from within, from without. It is well ordered too, that this happens when Paul is present, that he may answer them. When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question.
commentaries.content_en: Mark how at every step of the right progress in respect of the Gentiles, the beginning is brought in as matter of necessity. Before this (Peter) being found fault with, justified himself, and said all that he said in the tone of apology, which was what made his words acceptable: then, the Jews having turned away, upon this (Paul) came to the Gentiles. Here again, seeing another extravagance coming in, upon this (the apostle) enacts the law. For as it is likely that they, as being taught of God, discoursed to all indifferently, this moved to jealousy them of the Jews (who had believed). And they did not merely speak of circumcision, but they said, You cannot even be saved. Whereas the very opposite to this was the case, that receiving circumcision they could not be saved. Do you mark how closely the trials succeed each other, from within, from without. It is well ordered too, that this happens when Paul is present, that he may answer them. When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question.
commentaries.content_pt: Observe como, a cada passo do progresso correto em relação aos gentios, o início é trazido como questão de necessidade. Antes disso, (Pedro), sendo censurado, justificou-se e disse tudo o que disse em tom de desculpa, o que tornou suas palavras aceitáveis: então, os judeus se afastando, sobre isso (Paulo) veio aos gentios. Aqui novamente, vendo outra extravagância surgindo, sobre isso (o apóstolo) promulga a lei. Pois é provável que eles, sendo ensinados por Deus, discursassem a todos de maneira indiferente, o que provocou ciúmes nos judeus (que haviam crido). E não apenas falaram sobre a circuncisão, mas disseram: Vocês não podem sequer ser salvos. Enquanto que o oposto a isso era o caso, que ao receber a circuncisão, eles não podiam ser salvos. Você percebe como as provações se sucedem de forma tão próxima, de dentro, de fora. É bem ordenado também que isso aconteça quando Paulo está presente, para que ele possa responder a eles. Quando, portanto, Paulo e Barnabás tiveram não pequena dissensão e disputa com eles, determinaram que Paulo e Barnabás, e certos outros deles, deveriam subir a Jerusalém aos apóstolos e anciãos sobre esta questão.

ai_summary.one_sentence: Chrysostom analisa a tensão entre judeus e gentios na pregação de Paulo.
ai_summary.abstract: No comentário de João Crisóstomo, ele destaca a complexidade das interações entre os apóstolos e as comunidades judaicas e gentias. Ele observa que, após a crítica a Pedro, Paulo se torna a voz que responde às novas contestações sobre a circuncisão e a salvação. Crisóstomo enfatiza a necessidade de um diálogo contínuo e a importância de se buscar a verdade em meio a disputas. O autor também reflete sobre a natureza das provações enfrentadas pelos apóstolos e como essas experiências moldam a missão da Igreja primitiva. A análise revela a tensão entre a lei e a graça, bem como a necessidade de um entendimento mais profundo da salvação.
key_points: A crítica a Pedro e a justificação de suas ações.
A disputa entre judeus e gentios sobre a circuncisão.
A importância do papel de Paulo em resolver controvérsias.
A relação entre provações internas e externas na Igreja.
A necessidade de diálogo e esclarecimento teológico.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A tensão entre judeus e gentios na Igreja primitiva revela a complexidade da salvação e a necessidade de diálogo.
arguments: Pedro, ao ser criticado, busca justificar suas ações, mostrando a fragilidade das certezas humanas.
Paulo e Barnabás enfrentam oposição, o que evidencia a necessidade de uma autoridade central em Jerusalém para resolver disputas.
A circuncisão é um ponto de contenda que simboliza a luta entre a lei e a graça.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: A resolução dessas disputas é fundamental para a unidade da Igreja e a compreensão da salvação.

spiritual_insight.theme: A unidade na diversidade é essencial para a missão da Igreja.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Devemos buscar o diálogo e a compreensão em meio a diferenças, refletindo o amor de Cristo em nossas interações.

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: And certain men who came down from Judea taught the brethren, and said, Except you be circumcised after the manner of Moses, you cannot be saved. (b) But let us look more closely at what has been said. And certain men, etc. In Jerusalem, then, there were not any believers from among the Gentiles: but in Antioch of course there were. Therefore there came down certain yet laboring under this disease of the love of rule, and wishing to have those of the Gentiles attached to them. And yet Paul, though he too was learned in the Law, was not thus affected. When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small disputation with them, etc.
commentaries.content_en: And certain men who came down from Judea taught the brethren, and said, Except you be circumcised after the manner of Moses, you cannot be saved. (b) But let us look more closely at what has been said. And certain men, etc. In Jerusalem, then, there were not any believers from among the Gentiles: but in Antioch of course there were. Therefore there came down certain yet laboring under this disease of the love of rule, and wishing to have those of the Gentiles attached to them. And yet Paul, though he too was learned in the Law, was not thus affected. When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small disputation with them, etc.
commentaries.content_pt: E certos homens que desceram da Judeia ensinaram os irmãos, dizendo: Se não fores circuncidado segundo o rito de Moisés, não podes ser salvo. (b) Mas vamos olhar mais de perto o que foi dito. E certos homens, etc. Em Jerusalém, portanto, não havia crentes entre os gentios: mas em Antioquia, é claro, havia. Portanto, desceram certos que ainda padeciam dessa doença do amor ao domínio, desejando que os gentios se unissem a eles. E, no entanto, Paulo, embora também fosse versado na Lei, não foi assim afetado. Quando, portanto, Paulo e Barnabé tiveram não pequena disputa com eles, etc.

ai_summary.one_sentence: João Crisóstomo analisa a disputa sobre a circuncisão entre os cristãos gentios e judeus.
ai_summary.abstract: Neste comentário, João Crisóstomo discute a controvérsia surgida em Antioquia, onde alguns homens da Judeia insistiam que a circuncisão era necessária para a salvação dos gentios. Ele observa que, enquanto em Jerusalém não havia crentes gentios, em Antioquia a presença deles era evidente. Crisóstomo destaca a motivação dos que defendiam a circuncisão, que era o desejo de domínio sobre os gentios. Ele contrasta essa posição com a de Paulo, que, apesar de seu conhecimento da Lei, não se deixou levar por essa mentalidade. A análise de Crisóstomo revela a tensão entre a tradição judaica e a nova fé cristã, enfatizando a importância da graça sobre a lei.
key_points: A circuncisão como requisito para a salvação é contestada.
A presença de gentios em Antioquia é um ponto central na discussão.
Paulo é apresentado como um defensor da liberdade cristã.
A disputa reflete a tensão entre judeus e gentios na Igreja primitiva.
Crisóstomo critica a busca de domínio sobre os gentios.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A salvação não depende da circuncisão, mas da fé em Cristo.
arguments: A circuncisão era uma prática judaica que não se aplicava aos gentios convertidos.
Paulo, mesmo sendo judeu, defende a liberdade dos gentios em Cristo.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: A verdadeira salvação é alcançada pela fé, não pela observância da Lei.

spiritual_insight.theme: A salvação é um dom da graça, não uma conquista humana.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Devemos lembrar que nossa aceitação diante de Deus não depende de obras, mas da fé em Cristo.

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Mark how at every step of the right progress in respect of the Gentiles, the beginning is brought in as matter of necessity. Before this (Peter) being found fault with, justified himself, and said all that he said in the tone of apology, which was what made his words acceptable: then, the Jews having turned away, upon this (Paul) came to the Gentiles. Here again, seeing another extravagance coming in, upon this (the apostle) enacts the law. For as it is likely that they, as being taught of God, discoursed to all indifferently, this moved to jealousy them of the Jews (who had believed). And they did not merely speak of circumcision, but they said, Ye cannot even be saved. Whereas the very opposite to this was the case, that receiving circumcision they could not be saved. Do you mark how closely the trials succeed each other, from within, from without. It is well ordered too, that this happens when Paul is present, that he may answer them. "When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question. ) And Paul does not say, What. Have I not a right to be believed after so many signs. but he complied for their sakes. "And being brought on their way by the Church, they passed through Phenice and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles: and they caused great joy unto all the brethren. ) And observe, the consequence is that all the Samaritans also, learn what has come to the Gentiles: and they rejoiced. "And when they were come to Jerusalem, they were received of the Church, and of the apostles and elders, and they declared all things that God had done with them. ) See what a providence is here. "But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses. And the apostles and elders came together to consider of this matter. And when there had been much disputing, Peter rose up and said unto them, Men and brethren, ye know how that of old days God made choice among us, that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the Gospel, and believe. ) Observe Peter from the first standing aloof (kechorismenon) from the affair, and even to this time judaizing. And yet (says he) "ye know. ) Perhaps those were present who of old found fault with him in the matter of Cornelius, and went in with him (on that occasion): for this reason he brings them forward as witnesses. "From old days," he says, "did choose among you. " What means, "Among you. " Either, in Palestine, or, you being present. "By my mouth. " Observe how he shows that it was God speaking by him, and no human utterance. "And God, that knoweth the hearts, gave testimony unto them:" he refers them to the spiritual testimony: "by giving them the Holy Ghost even as unto us. ) Everywhere he puts the Gentiles upon a thorough equality. "And put no difference between us and them, having purified their hearts by faith. ) From faith alone, he says, they obtained the same gifts. This is also meant as a lesson to those (objectors); this is able to teach even them that faith only is needed, not works nor circumcision. For indeed they do not say all this only by way of apology for the Gentiles, but to teach (the Jewish believers) also to abandon the Law. However, at present this is not said. "Now therefore why tempt ye God, to put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples. ) What means, "Tempt ye God. " As if He had not power to save by faith. Consequently, it proceeds from a want of faith, this bringing in the Law. Then he shows that they themselves were nothing benefited by it, and he turns the whole (stress of his speech) against the Law, not against them, and (so) cuts short the accusation of them: "which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear. But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved, even as they. ) How full of power these words. The same that Paul says at large in the Epistle to the Romans, the same says Peter here. "For if Abraham," says (Paul), "was justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God. " (Romans 4:2. ) Do you perceive that all this is more a lesson for them than apology for the Gentiles. However, if he had spoken this without a plea for speaking, he would have been suspected: an occasion having offered, he lays hold of it, and speaks out fearlessly. See on all occasions how the designs of their foes are made to work with them. If those had not stirred the question, these things would not have been spoken, nor what follows. (Recapitulation. ) (b) But let us look more closely at what has been said. "And certain men," etc. In Jerusalem, then, there were not any believers from among the Gentiles: but in Antioch of course there were. Therefore there came down certain yet laboring under this disease of the love of rule, and wishing to have those of the Gentiles attached to them. And yet Paul, though he too was learned in the Law, was not thus affected. "When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small disputation with them," etc. ) But when he returned from thence, the doctrine also became more exact. For if they at Jerusalem enjoin no such thing, much more these (have no right to do so). "And being brought on their way," etc, "they caused no small joy to the brethren. ) Do you mark, as many as are not enamoured of rule, rejoiced in their believing. It was no ambitious feeling that prompted their recitals, neither was it for display, but in justification of the preaching to the Gentiles. ) Thus they say nothing of what had happened in the matter of the Jews. "But there arose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed," etc. ) (a) But even if they would needs bring over the Gentiles to their side, they learn that neither must the Apostles overlook it. "And the Apostles and elders," etc. ) "Among us," he says, "God chose:" and "from old days:" long ago, he says, not now. And this too is no small point--at a time when Jews believed, not turned away (from the Gospel). "Among us;" an argument from the place: "of old days," from the time. And that expression, "Chose:" just as in their own case he says not, (so) willed it, but, "Chose that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the Gospel and believe. " Whence is this proved. From the Spirit. Then he shows that the testimony given them is not of grace merely, but of their virtue. "And God which knoweth the hearts bare them witness" (v. 8); having afforded to them nothing less (than to us), for, he says, "Put no difference between us and them. ) Why then, hearts are what one must everywhere look to. And it is very appositely said, "God that knoweth the hearts bare them witness:" as in the former instance, "Thou, Lord, that knowest the hearts of all men. ) For to show that this is the meaning, observe what he adds, "Put no difference between us and them. " When he has mentioned the testimony borne to them, then he utters that great word, the same which Paul speaks, "Neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision. " (1 Corinthians 7:19. ) "That he may make the twain one in Himself. " (Ephesians 2:5. ) Of all these the seeds lie in Peter's discourse. And he does not say (between) them of the circumcision, but "Between us," that is the Apostles, "and them. " Then, that the expression, "no difference" may not seem an outrage, After faith, he says--"Having purified their hearts by faith" (v. 10)--He thoroughly cleansed them first. Then he shows, not that the Law was evil, but themselves weak. --"But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved even as they. ) Mark how he ends with a fearful consideration. He does not discourse to them from the Prophets, but from things present, of which themselves were witnesses. Of course (the Prophets) also themselves anon add their testimony (infra v. 15), and make the reason stronger by what has now come to pass. And observe, he first permits the question to be moved in the Church, and then speaks. "And put no difference between"--he said not, them of the circumcision, but "us and them," i. the Gentiles: for this (gradual advance) little by little is stronger. "Why therefore tempt ye God. " who is become (the) God of the Gentiles: for this was tempting: * * * whether He is able to save even after the Law. See what he does. He shows that they are in danger. For if, what the Law could not do, faith had power to do, "we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved even as they" (comp. Galatians 2:16): but faith falling off, behold, themselves (are) in destruction. And he did not say, Why do ye disbelieve. which was more harsh, but, "Tempt God," and that when the fact is demonstrated. (c) Great effrontery this, of the Pharisees, that even after faith they set up the Law, and will not obey the Apostles. But see these, how mildly they speak, and not in the tone of authority: such words are amiable, and more apt to fix themselves in the mind. Observe, it is nowhere a display of words, but demonstration by facts, by the Spirit. And yet, though they have such proofs, they still speak gently. And observe they do not come accusing those at Antioch, but "declaring all things that God had done with them:" (v. 4) but thence again these men lay hold upon the occasion (to compass their own objects), "but there rose up," etc. ) Such were the pains they took in their love of power: and it was not with the knowledge of the Apostles that they Paul and Barnabas were blamed. But still they brought forward none of these charges: but when they have proved the matter, then (the Apostles) write in stronger terms. For gentleness is everywhere a great good: gentleness, I say, not stupid indifference; gentleness, not adulation: for between these there is a vast difference. Nothing ruffled Paul, nothing discomposed Peter. When thou hast convincing proofs, why lose thy temper, to render these of none effect. It is impossible for one who is out of temper ever to persuade. Yesterday also we discoursed about anger; but there is no reason why we should not to-day also; perchance a second exhortation coming directly after the first will effect somewhat. For indeed a medicine though of virtue to heal a wound, unless it be constantly renewed, mars all. And think not that our continual discoursing about the same things is a condemning of you: for if we condemned you, we should not discourse; but now, hoping that you will gain much, we speak these things. Would indeed that we did speak constantly of the same things: would that there were no other subject of our discourses, than how we might overcome our passions. For is it not contrary to all reason, that while emperors, living in luxury and so great honor, have no subject of discourse either while sitting at table, or at any other time, save only how to overcome their enemies --and therefore it is that they hold their assemblies each day, and appoint generals and soldiers, and demand taxes and tributes; and that of all state affairs, the moving causes are these two, the overcoming of those who make war upon them, and the establishing of their subjects in peace--we have no mind for such themes as this, nor ever even dream of conversing upon them: but how we may buy land, or purchase slaves, and make our property greater, these are subjects we can talk about every day, and never be tired of them: while concerning things in ourselves and really our own, we neither wish to speak ourselves, nor so much as dream of tolerating advice, nor of enduring to hear others speaking about them. But answer me, what do you talk about. About dinner. Why that is a subject for cooks. Nay, that is a theme for hucksters and merchants. Of buildings. That belongs to carpenters and builders. That talk is for husbandmen. But for us, there is no other proper business, save this, how we may make wealth for the soul. Then let not the discourse be wearisome to you. Why is it that none finds fault with the physician for always discoursing of the healing art, nor with people of other crafts for talking about their peculiar arts. If indeed the mastery over our passions were really achieved, so that there were no need of putting us in mind, we might reasonably be taxed with ambition and display: or rather, not then either. For even if it were gained, for all that, there would be need of discoursing, that one might not relapse and remain uncorrected: as in fact physicians discourse not only to the sick, but also to the whole, and they have books on this subject, on the one part how to free from disease, on the other how to preserve health. So that even if we are well, still we must not give over, but must do all in order to the preserving of our health. And when we are sick there is a twofold necessity for advice: first, that we may be freed from the disease; secondly, that having been freed, we may not fall into it again. Well then, we are discoursing now by the method of treating the sick, not by the rules for the treatment of the healthy. How then may one root out this evil passion. how subdue (huposkeliseie) this violent fever. Let us see whence it had its birth, and let us remove the cause. Whence is it wont to arise. From arrogance and much haughtiness. This cause then let us remove, and the disease is removed together with it. But what is arrogance. whence does it arise. for perhaps we are likely to have to go back to a still higher origin. But whatever course the reason of the thing may point out, that let us take, that we may go to the bottom of the mischief, and pluck it up by the roots. Whence then comes arrogance. From our not looking into our own concerns, but instead of that, busying ourselves about the nature of land, though we are not husbandmen, and the nature of gold, though we are not merchants, and concerning clothing, and everything else: while to ourselves and our own nature we never look at all. And who, you will say, is ignorant of his own nature. Many: perhaps all, save a few: and if ye will, I will show the proof of it. For, tell me, what is man. If one were asked, will he be able to answer outright to the questions, In what he differs from the brutes, in what he is akin to the heavenly inhabitants, what can be made of man. For as in the case of any other material, so also in this case: man is the subject-matter, but of this can be made either an angel or a beast. Does not this seem a strange saying. And yet ye have often heard it in the Scriptures. For of certain human beings it was said, "he is the angel of the Lord" (Malachi 2:7): and "from his lips," saith it, "they shall seek judgment" (Malachi 3:1): and again, "I send My angel before Thy face:" but of some, "Serpents, generation of vipers. " (Matthew 12:34. ) So then, it all depends upon the use. Why do I say, an angel. the man can become God, and a child of God. For we read, "I have said, Ye are gods, and all of you are children of the Most High. " (Psalm 82:6. ) And what is greater, the power to become both God and angel and child of God is put into his own hands. Yea, so it is, man can be the maker of an angel. Perchance this saying has startled you. Hear however Christ saying: "In the Resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like unto the angels. " (Matthew 22:30. ) And again, "He that is able to receive it, let him receive it. " (Matthew 19:12. ) In a word, it is virtue which makes angels: but this is in our power: therefore we are able to make angels, though not in nature, certainly in will. For indeed if virtue be absent, it is no advantage to be an angel by nature; and the Devil is a proof of this, who was an angel once: but if virtue be present, it is no loss to be a man by nature; and John is a proof of this, who was a man, and Elias who went up into heaven, and all those who are about to depart thither. For these indeed, though with bodies, were not prevented from dwelling in heaven: while those others, though without bodies, could not remain in heaven. Let no one then grieve or be vexed with his nature as if it were a hindrance to him, but with his will. He (the Devil) from being incorporeal became a lion: for lo. it saith, "Our adversary, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour" (1 Pet. 8): we from being corporeal, become angels. For just as if a person, having found some precious material, should despise it, as not being an artificer, it will be a great loss to him, whether it be pearls, or a pearl shell, or any other such thing that he has seen; so we likewise, if we are ignorant of our own nature, shall despise it much: but if we know what it is, we shall exhibit much zeal, and reap the greatest profits. For from this nature is wrought a king's robe, from this a king's house, from this nature are fashioned a king's members: all are kingly. Let us not then misuse our own nature to our hurt. He has made us "a little lower than the angels," (Psalm 8:5), I mean, by reason of death: but even that little we have now recovered. There is nothing therefore to hinder us from becoming nigh to the angels, if we will. Let us then will it, let us will it, and having exercised ourselves thoroughly, let us return honor to the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit, now and ever, world without end, Amen.

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content: The reason why the Holy Spirit did, when the apostles at that time were consulting, relax the bond and yoke for us,
commentaries.content_en: The reason why the Holy Spirit did, when the apostles at that time were consulting, relax the bond and yoke for us,
commentaries.content_pt: A razão pela qual o Espírito Santo, quando os apóstolos naquele momento estavam consultando, relaxou o vínculo e o jugo para nós,

ai_summary.one_sentence: Tertuliano explica a liberdade do Espírito Santo em relaxar as restrições para os apóstolos.
ai_summary.abstract: No comentário de Tertuliano, ele aborda a ação do Espírito Santo durante um momento crucial em que os apóstolos estavam deliberando sobre questões importantes. Tertuliano sugere que o Espírito Santo, em sua sabedoria, decidiu relaxar as restrições que antes eram impostas, permitindo uma nova compreensão e liberdade na prática da fé. Essa ação é vista como um sinal da adaptabilidade do Espírito Santo às necessidades da Igreja primitiva, refletindo a dinâmica entre a tradição e a revelação contínua.
key_points: O Espírito Santo atua de forma dinâmica na Igreja.
A liberdade concedida aos apóstolos reflete uma nova era na prática cristã.
A relação entre tradição e revelação é enfatizada.

argumentative_structure.thesis: O Espírito Santo tem a autoridade para modificar as práticas da Igreja conforme necessário.
arguments: A ação do Espírito Santo é uma demonstração de sua soberania sobre a Igreja.
As decisões dos apóstolos são guiadas pela inspiração do Espírito, que relaxa restrições anteriores.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: Portanto, a liberdade concedida pelo Espírito Santo é essencial para a evolução da prática cristã.

spiritual_insight.theme: A flexibilidade do Espírito Santo é um convite à adaptação na fé.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Reconhecer a ação do Espírito Santo nos encoraja a ser abertos a mudanças e novas interpretações na vida cristã.

commentaries.author: The Apostolic Constitutions
commentaries.content: For certain men likewise went down from Judea to Antioch, and taught the brethren who were there, saying: "Unless ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, and walk according to the other customs which he ordained, ye cannot be saved."
commentaries.content_en: For certain men likewise went down from Judea to Antioch, and taught the brethren who were there, saying: "Unless ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, and walk according to the other customs which he ordained, ye cannot be saved."
commentaries.content_pt: Porque certos homens desceram da Judeia para Antioquia e ensinaram os irmãos que ali estavam, dizendo: "Se não fores circuncidados segundo o modo de Moisés, e não andardes conforme os outros costumes que ele ordenou, não podereis ser salvos."

ai_summary.one_sentence: Os ensinamentos sobre a circuncisão e a salvação refletem tensões na Igreja primitiva.
ai_summary.abstract: O comentário das Constituições Apostólicas destaca a controvérsia sobre a necessidade da circuncisão e da observância da lei mosaica para a salvação. Este debate é central na história da Igreja primitiva, especialmente entre os cristãos de origem judaica e os gentios. A insistência em práticas judaicas como condição para a salvação levanta questões sobre a natureza da graça e da fé. O versículo reflete a tensão entre a tradição judaica e a nova aliança em Cristo, enfatizando a necessidade de discernimento na prática da fé cristã.
key_points: A circuncisão como símbolo da antiga aliança.
A tensão entre judeus e gentios na Igreja primitiva.
A questão da salvação pela fé versus obras da lei.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A salvação não depende da observância da lei mosaica, mas da fé em Cristo.
arguments: A circuncisão era um sinal da antiga aliança, mas não é necessária para a nova aliança em Cristo.
A salvação é pela graça mediante a fé, conforme ensinado por Paulo em suas epístolas.
objections: Alguns podem argumentar que a observância da lei é essencial para manter a identidade judaica.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: A Igreja deve reconhecer que a verdadeira salvação vem pela fé em Cristo, e não pela observância da lei.

spiritual_insight.theme: A liberdade em Cristo supera as exigências da lei.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Devemos confiar na graça de Deus para a salvação, em vez de depender de obras ou tradições humanas.
